title: Afghanistan-Pakistan War Update — Mar 29, 2026
summary: Pakistan formally resumed Operation Ghazab-il-Haq on March 26 following the Eid ceasefire collapse, with artillery strikes continuing in Kunar, Khost, Paktika and Nangarhar provinces. Human Rights Watch declared Pakistan's March 16 airstrike on the Omid Drug Rehabilitation Center in Kabul unlawful and a possible war crime, as the revised death toll reached 411 killed. A mass funeral was held in Kabul on March 26 with Interior Minister Haqqani vowing revenge. The Torkham border crossing briefly reopened for deportations on March 26 before being closed again after an Afghan sniper wounded a Pakistani Frontier Corps officer on the night of March 27. UNHCR confirmed 115,000 Afghans have been displaced since February 26, with no new ceasefire framework in sight.

title: Afghanistan-Pakistan War Update — Mar 26, 2026
summary: The five-day Eid al-Fitr ceasefire between Pakistan and Afghanistan, brokered by Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and Turkey, collapsed on March 24 as cross-border artillery exchanges resumed within minutes of its expiry in Kunar and Zabul provinces, killing at least two Afghan civilians. Pakistan's Foreign Minister Ishaq Dar signaled no change in strategic posture, reaffirming commitment to 'eradicate terrorism' and leaving the door open to future military operations. The TTP simultaneously ended its own three-day Eid ceasefire, resuming attacks inside Khyber Pakhtunkhwa. The UN has documented at least 289 civilian deaths in Afghanistan and 115,000 displaced since fighting intensified on February 26, following Pakistan's retaliatory airstrikes — including the March 16 strike on the Omid drug rehabilitation center in Kabul that killed at least 143 people. No new mediation framework is currently active.
